title: Apartments for Rent in Cleveland Under $900
metaTitle: Apartments for Rent in Cleveland Under $900
metaDescription: Find apartments for rent in Cleveland under $900 a month. See real rent data by unit size, affordable neighborhoods, and Section 8-ready homes. Book a showing.
answerBox: Yes, apartments renting under $900 a month exist in Cleveland, Ohio, mostly among smaller or older units outside large managed complexes. Citywide medians run higher — Zumper's July 4, 2026 report shows $1,060 for studios and $1,195 for one-bedrooms — but individual listings, especially east-side rental homes, regularly fall below $900.

sections.h2: Are there apartments for rent in Cleveland under $900 a month?
body: Yes. Cleveland's rental market runs well below the national average overall, and units priced under $900 a month are common among smaller, older buildings and rental homes outside the city's largest professionally managed complexes. Zumper's July 4, 2026 report puts Cleveland's citywide median rent at $1,250 and the national median at $1,950 — about 36% higher — which is the affordability gap that makes sub-$900 listings realistic here in a way they often aren't in larger metros.

That said, published medians and averages describe the middle of the market, not the floor. A meaningful share of individual units, particularly smaller 1- and 2-bedroom homes on the city's east side, list below those citywide figures.

sections.h2: What Cleveland's rent data shows by unit size
body: Here's how Cleveland rents break down by unit size across the two most-cited sources: Zumper's July 4, 2026 median-asking-rent report, and RentCafe's July 2, 2026 average-rent report for larger, professionally managed buildings. The RentCafe figures run higher because they track newer, amenity-heavy communities; the Zumper medians reflect the fuller mix of listings, including older and smaller buildings where sub-$900 rents are more likely.

sections.h2: What our under-$900 rental homes look like
body: Across our own selection of 90+ rental homes in Greater Cleveland, rents range from about $700 to $1,800 a month, with a typical rent near $1,000. The most affordable homes in that range tend to be smaller 1-bedroom and 2-bedroom units, and recent 2-bedroom availability has started as low as roughly $750 a month. Because availability shifts week to week, the honest way to find out what's currently under $900 is to [book a showing](/p/book-showing) and tell us your budget — we'll point you to what's actually open rather than quote a number that may already be gone.

Our current selection is mostly multi-family housing rather than large apartment complexes — duplexes, triplexes, and fourplexes make up the majority of what we work with, alongside a smaller share of true single-family houses. A sub-$900 rental from us is more likely to be one unit inside a duplex or fourplex than a standalone house, simply because smaller units tend to carry lower rent.

sections.h2: Where lower-cost rentals tend to be in Cleveland
body: Our lower-priced rental homes are concentrated in East and Southeast Cleveland ZIP codes, including 44105 (Slavic Village/Union-Miles, near the Morgana Run Trail and I-77/I-490), 44110 (Collinwood/Nottingham, on the city's northeast lakefront side), and 44108 (Glenville, a short distance from University Circle and served by nearby RTA bus routes). We also manage a smaller number of homes on the West side around 44102 (Cudell/Detroit-Shoreway, near the Gordon Square Arts District) and 44109 (Old Brooklyn/Brooklyn Centre, near the Cleveland Metroparks Zoo). We don't have rental homes in every Cleveland ZIP code, so book a showing to confirm what's available near a specific area.

A rent number under $900 doesn't tell the whole story of monthly housing cost. Renters should also budget for utilities — electric through either Cleveland Public Power or the Illuminating Company depending on the address, natural gas mostly through Enbridge Gas Ohio, and water through Cleveland Water — plus renters insurance and, in winter months, higher heating bills, since Cleveland sits in a lake-effect snow belt that runs heaviest in the city's higher-elevation east-side areas roughly from November through January. A common rule of thumb landlords use nationally is that monthly rent should run at or under about a third of gross income, which is worth checking against a target budget before committing to a lease, even at the lower end of Cleveland's price range.

A Housing Choice Voucher from the **Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority (CMHA)** covers rent up to a set payment standard for the household's bedroom size, with the tenant generally paying the difference — which can make a higher-priced unit affordable even if it's above $900 on paper. Every home we work with accepts Section 8 vouchers and is HUD-inspection-ready, regardless of price. Ohio has no statewide source-of-income protection law, and the City of Cleveland does not currently require landlords to accept vouchers, so always confirm a landlord's policy directly. Check [CMHA's Housing Choice Voucher page](https://www.cmha.net/housing/applicants/housing_choice_vouchers.php) for current income limits and payment standards, or read our full [Cleveland Section 8 guide](/section-8-housing-cleveland-oh/). For a broader look at pricing across the city, see [apartments for rent in Cleveland](/apartments-for-rent-cleveland-oh/) or compare with [cheap apartments for rent in Cleveland](/cleveland-rentals/apartments/cheap-apartments-for-rent-cleveland/).

faq.q: Can you find an apartment in Cleveland for under $900 a month?
faq.a: Yes. While citywide medians run higher — Zumper's July 4, 2026 report shows $1,060 for studios and $1,195 for one-bedrooms — individual listings, especially smaller or older units and east-side rental homes, regularly fall below $900. Availability changes often, so check current listings rather than relying on a fixed number.

faq.q: Does Rent Finder Cleveland have apartments under $900?
faq.a: Our current selection's rents range from about $700 to $1,800 a month, and our smaller 1- and 2-bedroom units are the most likely to fall under $900. Book a showing and tell us your budget, and we'll confirm what's currently available.

faq.q: Which Cleveland neighborhoods tend to have lower rent?
faq.a: Our more affordable rental homes are concentrated in East and Southeast Cleveland ZIP codes like 44105 (Slavic Village), 44110 (Collinwood), and 44108 (Glenville), with a smaller number of homes on the West side around 44102 and 44109.

faq.q: Can I use a Section 8 voucher on an apartment under $900 in Cleveland?
faq.a: Yes. A Housing Choice Voucher can be used on any rent level, including units under $900, and every home we work with accepts vouchers and is HUD-inspection-ready. Check CMHA's current payment standards, since your voucher's subsidy amount depends on household size and bedroom count.

faq.q: Why do rent averages look higher than $900 if cheaper units exist?
faq.a: Published averages and medians, like RentCafe's $1,818 two-bedroom average, weight toward newer, professionally managed communities. Zumper's medians and our own selection data show that smaller, older units and rental homes — especially on Cleveland's east side — frequently list well below those figures.

faq.q: What other costs should I budget for besides rent under $900?
faq.a: Factor in utilities (electric, gas, and water providers vary by address), renters insurance, and higher winter heating costs, since Cleveland's east side sits in a lake-effect snow belt roughly from November through January. A common guideline is keeping total rent at or under about a third of gross monthly income.
